title: UConn, South Carolina, Texas, UCLA All Reach Women's Final Four In 2nd Straight Season
summary: The 2026 Women's Final Four is a complete repeat of the 2025 edition with UConn, UCLA, Texas and South Carolina all winning their respective regions as the No. 1 seeds. This is the second time that the identical four teams have reached the semifinals in consecutive seasons when UConn, Tennessee, Stanford and Georgia were in the Final Four in 1995 and 1996. Geno Auriemma's UConn team has reached the Final Four in 17 out of the past 18 seasons, winning seven national championships over that timespan. Dawn Staley's South Carolina has now been to six straight Final Fours, which is the longest streak in the country. UConn and South Carolina, who will meet in the semifinals on Friday, have a 3-3 record against each other since 2021. The Huskies defeated South Carolina 82-59 in last year's national championship game. Previously, South Carolina defeated UConn 64-49 in the 2022 national championship game.

title: Joe Mazzulla Calls Coach Of The Year Award 'Dumb,' Credits Players, Staff With Surprise Season
summary: Boston Celtics coach Joe Mazzulla wants no part of Coach of the Year conversations, delivering a characteristically blunt assessment of the award Monday despite being considered one of the leading candidates. "I don't need it," Mazzulla said. "I think it's a stupid award. They shouldn't have it. And it's more about the players. It's more about the work that the staff puts in. It's just that simple. I really don't ever want to be asked or talk about it again. It's just that dumb. So, the players play. It's about them. Staff works their ass off. I'm grateful to have them." Mazzulla's candidacy is well founded regardless of his indifference. He has guided Boston to its fifth consecutive 50-win season through a significant offseason roster overhaul and Jayson Tatum missing most of the year with a torn Achilles. The Celtics currently sit second in the Eastern Conference. Atlanta Hawks coach Quin Snyder offered an expansive endorsement of Mazzulla's work. "Anytime your roster changes, when you lose a player of Jayson Tatum's caliber, you adjust," Snyder said. "What Joe's done is figure out a way to play that maximizes the personality that they have. And I think ultimately that's fundamentally, when I look at them and watch them play, why I would say that he's done such a terrific job."

title: Jaden Ivey Waived By Bulls After Anti-LGTBQ Comments
summary: Jaden Ivey will be waived by the Chicago Bulls following his recent anti-LGBTQ comments on social media. The Bulls acquired Ivey from the Detroit Pistons ahead of the trade deadline and was set to be a restricted free agent in the offseason. Ivey was recently shut down for the season by the Bulls with a knee injury. "The world proclaims LGBTQ, right? They proclaim Pride Month and the NBA does too. They show it to the world. They say, 'Come join us for Pride Month to celebrate unrighteousness,'" said Ivey. Ivey went on Instagram Live, where he continued to talk about religion and the NBA. "They're liars, bro. This is lying," Ivey said. "They're lying saying my conduct is detrimental to the team. That's a lie. Ask any one of them coaches in there, 'Was I a good teammate?' All I'm preaching about is Jesus Christ and they waived me. They say I'm crazy, right? I'm psycho." He later said, "I didn't get myself waived. I was in the gym today. I was rehabbing, doing what was required of my job." Billy Donovan responded to the decision to part ways with Ivey on Monday. "I think there's a certain level of standards and expectations that are here," Donovan said. "I mean, we have people from all different walks of life working in the building and players from all different walks of life, right? So, the first thing is, everybody comes with their own personal experiences. But one is, we've got to all be professional. I think there's got to be a high level of respect for one another, and we got to help each other and then be accountable to those standards."

title: Tom Dundon Group's $4.25 Billion Purchase Of Blazers Unanimously Approved By Board Of Governors
summary: The NBA Board of Governors has unanimously approved the sale of the Portland Trail Blazers to a group led by Dallas businessman Tom Dundon, a league source told The Athletic. The deal is structured at an approximate total value of $4.25 billion. The transaction ends the Allen family's ownership era dating to 1988. It is structured in two stages: an 80.1 percent stake closes March 31 at a $4 billion valuation, with the remaining 19.9 percent closing no later than September 1, 2028, at a $4.5 billion valuation. During the interim period, former Blazers chairman Bert Kolde will observe board meetings without governance authority. Dundon's purchasing group includes Portland's Sheel Tyle of investment firm Collective Global, Chicago's Marc Zahr of Blue Owl, the Cherng family of Las Vegas, founders of Panda Express, and Freedom Mortgage CEO Stanley Middleman, who also holds a partial stake in the Philadelphia Phillies. Dundon currently serves as majority owner of the NHL's Carolina Hurricanes, which he acquired in 2018 for $420 million. He recently sold 12.5 percent of that stake at a $2.66 billion valuation while retaining majority control. Carolina has reached the playoffs in eight straight seasons under his stewardship. The Blazers are seeking $600 million to renovate the Moda Center. Oregon's Legislature has already committed $365 million, funded through income tax revenues from team-related activity. Additional contributions from Multnomah County and the city of Portland remain pending council votes. Paul Allen purchased the franchise in 1988 for $70 million. Following his death in 2018, his sister Jody assumed governance. The Allen estate listed the team for sale in May 2025, with all proceeds designated for charitable distribution.

title: Bobby Portis Says Giannis Antetokounmpo Will Not Play Again This Season For Bucks
summary: Milwaukee Bucks forward Bobby Portis publicly stated that teammate Giannis Antetokounmpo will not return to the court this season, citing both the team's eliminated playoff status and broader business considerations surrounding a potential trade. Portis, entering his sixth season with Milwaukee, made the remarks during an appearance on FanDuel TV's "Run It Back." The Bucks were mathematically eliminated from postseason contention Saturday, sitting at 29-45 with eight games remaining. "I don't think he'll play another game this year, for sure," Portis said. "Just being smart as a pro, right now, I don't think it's smart for him to play." Antetokounmpo has suited up for just 36 games this season amid a string of injuries. He has missed the club's last seven contests following a left knee hyperextension. The Athletic previously reported he resisted the organization's suggestion to shut him down entirely. A dispute over his status has intensified in recent days. The National Basketball Players Association released a statement claiming Antetokounmpo is healthy and the Bucks are preventing his return . Doc Rivers directly contested that assertion , maintaining the star has not received medical clearance. Portis did not shy away from the trade speculation surrounding the two-time MVP. "If y'all talking about trading him, you don't want any injury to knock value off or whatever," Portis said. "That's just business-wise, and then player-wise, obviously, you gotta look at just the total picture of what's going on. We're 10 games behind for the 10th seed and (with) nine games left, we can't even make up ground." Bucks general manager Jon Horst fielded trade inquiries ahead of the February deadline before opting to retain Antetokounmpo. Renewed discussions are anticipated this offseason.

title: Mark Cuban Regrets Selling Mavericks To Adelson/Dumont Family
summary: Mark Cuban sold the Dallas Mavericks over two years ago and the franchise immediately went on to The Finals in 2024, but Nico Harrison's decision to trade Luka Doncic in February 2025 has created a tumultuous period for the franchise. Cuban was sidelined by Harrison, who reported directly to team governor Patrick Dumont. Cuban has been increasingly outspoken about the Mavericks and this week has said he regrets selling the Mavericks to Miriam Adelson. "I don't regret selling. I regret who I sold to," Cuban said in a teaser clip released Monday. "I made a lot of mistakes in the process, and I'll leave it at that." Cuban, who has been one of the NBA's most emotionally invested owners, has said he didn't want to burden his children with the responsibilities of owning the Mavericks. "I didn't want that for them. It can be abusive, a lot," Cuban said. "If fans don't like what you're doing or if the team's not doing well, you're the worst human being on the planet and they treat you that way." The Mavericks were given a lifeline to recover from the fallout of trading Doncic when they won the lottery last May and subsequently selected Cooper Flagg. Harrison was fired by the Mavericks in November and the front office is currently being run by Michael Finley and Matt Riccardi on an interim basis. When Cuban sold the Mavericks, he had expected to run basketball operations "forevermore ."

title: Arizona, Michigan Co-Favorites At +175 To Win NCAA Tournament
summary: Arizona and Michigan enter the Final Four as co-favorites to win the NCAA men's basketball tournament at +175 , according to DraftKings. Illinois (+400) and UConn (+550) round out the field as long shots. Both Arizona and Michigan entered March Madness with strong odds, +380 and +370, respectively. Illinois and UConn were far longer shots at 22-1 and 25-1 entering the tournament. Duke, the original favorite at +360, was eliminated on a buzzer-beating three-pointer by UConn. One Caesars Sportsbook bettor in New Jersey had $300,000 riding on UConn pregame at +205 odds. "We will be rooting for the winner of the Arizona-Michigan semifinal to take care of business in the final. We've spent all season trying to build a good position on these teams (and Duke, but that's another story), and hopefully it will pay off," Circa Sports race & sportsbook manager Chase Michaelson told ESPN. "I would consider Arizona or Michigan excellent results. Illinois is bad, UConn is worse." BetMGM senior trader Michael Ranftle identified UConn as the book's sole remaining liability. The sportsbook accepted a $25,000 wager on UConn at +900 and a $50,000 wager on Arizona at +450, each worth $225,000 if cashed. FanDuel reports UConn has drawn the highest volume of championship bets overall and in the past 24 hours. BetMGM's ideal outcome is a Michigan title, with Arizona or Illinois also favorable results. "This is as close as it can get," Michaelson said. "These are two of the best teams we've seen in quite some time, in terms of a mix of NBA talent, college experience and coaching... In most years, either of these teams would likely be odds-on favorites to win the title by this time, but they have to play each other." Michigan opened as a slight underdog but has since moved to -1.5 over Arizona. Illinois holds steady at -2.5 against UConn, though early ticket volume favors the Huskies.

title: EuroLeague's Hire Of Former NBA Exec Chus Bueno As CEO Fuels Optimism Over NBA Merger
summary: The EuroLeague's appointment of Chus Bueno as its new CEO has generated fresh optimism among league sources that the NBA and EuroLeague can reach a collaborative arrangement rather than compete directly against one another in Europe. Bueno is a former NBA executive who worked on league development initiatives across Europe, Africa and the Middle East. The shift in sentiment coincides with NBA Commissioner Adam Silver's most direct public statement yet on the league's preferred approach to European expansion, delivered Wednesday at the Board of Governors news conference. "I think for the betterment of European basketball," Silver said, "the best outcome would be if we came together with the EuroLeague here and that we came up with a systematic approach to growing the game throughout Europe." The NBA has been working toward launching its own European league targeting a 2027-28 start, with planned franchises in major cities across England, France, Germany, Italy and Spain. The external bid process for franchises is expected to close by month's end. The EuroLeague currently includes 13 licensed shareholder clubs with long-term standing in the competition, which features 20 teams this season . Any NBA Europe structure is expected to accommodate 12 locked-in franchises with four annual qualifying spots, meaning not all 13 existing EuroLeague clubs could be absorbed. Including established powers such as Real Madrid and Barcelona, however, would significantly strengthen any combined venture's commercial appeal and credibility across the continent.

title: St. Bonaventure Hires Mike MacDonald From Division II Daemen As Head Coach
summary: St. Bonaventure is finalizing a deal to hire Daemen College coach Mike MacDonald as its next men's basketball head coach, a source briefed on the decision confirmed. The announcement is expected this week, continuing the program's pattern of outside-the-box appointments following its 2024 hire of ESPN insider Adrian Wojnarowski as general manager. MacDonald, 59, spent 12 seasons at Division II Daemen in Amherst, N.Y., compiling a 61-3 record over the past two years. This season's Daemen squad finished 33-2 before falling in the Division II Elite Eight. He replaces Mark Schmidt, who retired after 19 seasons, a program-record 341 victories and three NCAA Tournament appearances. St. Bonaventure parted ways with Schmidt with one year left on his contract after the team stumbled to a 4-14 Atlantic 10 finish following an 11-2 start, a decision that drew criticism from fans who felt Schmidt deserved a more dignified departure. Athletic director Bob Beretta acknowledged the controversy in a March 17 open letter, describing the program as being at an "inflection point" and calling for a leader capable of maximizing resources in the NIL era. MacDonald is expected to be among the Atlantic 10's lowest-paid coaches, a structure that could allow St. Bonaventure to direct additional funds toward its roster to compete in the NIL era. The model pairs a low-profile coach with Wojnarowski, whose extensive NBA connections could prove valuable in recruiting. Earlier in his career, MacDonald compiled a 108-153 record over nine seasons at Canisius, where he replaced John Beilein as head coach in 1997 after serving on his staff. The hiring reflects a broader trend of Division II coaches earning opportunities at higher levels. Iowa's Ben McCollum, who won four national championships at Northwest Missouri State, has guided the Hawkeyes to the Elite Eight in his first season. Josh Schertz, who spent 13 years as a Division II head coach, led Saint Louis to the second round of this year's NCAA Tournament.

title: Jaylen Brown Calls Out 'Gap Year' Expectations As Celtics Win 50th Game
summary: After the Boston Celtics clinched a playoff berth with a 114-99 victory over the Charlotte Hornets on Sunday, Jaylen Brown posted a simple message on social media in response to those who wrote off their chances of accomplishing anything this season amid injury and roster turnover . "50 wins in a gap year," Brown wrote. The remark was a direct callback to preseason skepticism that followed Jayson Tatum's torn Achilles and the offseason trades of Jrue Holiday and Kristaps Porzingis. The Celtics also lost Luke Kornet and Al Horford to free agency. Boston has answered those doubts emphatically, reaching 50 wins for the fifth consecutive season and extending the NBA's longest active playoff streak to 12 years. Brown sat out Sunday's game with left Achilles tendinitis, but Tatum carried the offense with 32 points, his highest total in 11 games since returning from injury earlier this month. The Celtics have gone 9-2 since Tatum's return and have won seven of their past eight games, including a victory over the league-best Oklahoma City Thunder. Tatum's performance featured an array of precise footwork, decisive drives and patient playmaking against double-teams. He also became the youngest player in Celtics history to reach 14,000 career points. "I didn't feel like I was sped up," Tatum said. "I was still kind of tired, so that's something I'm still working through. But I like that I'm being decisive in my movements and exploding when I need to. I can say I feel better than I did last game." Tatum acknowledged the demanding nature of his rehabilitation process while expressing gratitude for his supporting cast. "I've had to work my [tail] off just to run up and down the floor," he said. "I've been fortunate enough to be on some really good teams, be surrounded by some really good players and coaches that have empowered me and helped me become the player I am today." With Brown leading what many consider an MVP-caliber season and Tatum trending upward, Boston has emerged as a legitimate championship contender heading into the postseason with the second best NBA championship betting odds behind the Oklahoma City Thunder. Brown ranks fifth in MVP odds , setting himself up for an excellent shot at being an All-NBA First Team selection.

title: Jamhal Mosley Accepts Responsibility After Magic Surrender 31-0 Run, 52-Point Loss To Raptors
summary: Orlando Magic head coach Jamahl Mosley pointed blame at himself Sunday after his team suffered the most lopsided loss in franchise history, a 139-87 defeat to the Toronto Raptors that included a 31-0 run spanning 7 minutes and 47 seconds, which is the longest unanswered scoring surge in the NBA's play-by-play era. "We've got to have them prepared for the physicality of the game, prepared for how much they were going to grab, hold and get them off their spots," Mosley said. "That's on me." "I've got to be able to do a better job of preparing them for what they were going to see tonight," he added. "We talked a little bit about it, but probably not enough." Orlando committed 28 turnovers, including 12 in the first quarter alone, tying a team record for most in any period. Toronto converted those miscues into 37 points and outscored the Magic 39-7 in fast-break points. The Magic trailed 70-43 at halftime. The defeat was a significant missed opportunity. Toronto was without starting guard Immanuel Quickley, leading scorer Brandon Ingram and two rotation players, yet handled Orlando with ease. The Magic entered the game two games behind the fifth-place Raptors in a crowded Eastern Conference standings race. Paolo Banchero declined to place blame solely on Mosley. "It's all a collective," Banchero said. "Gotta find ways to stop runs like that. We didn't. If you want to be a good team, you can't give up runs like that." Orlando has now dropped seven of eight games, including losses to the league-worst Indiana Pacers and the Charlotte Hornets. With eight games remaining, the Magic hold the eighth seed but risk falling to the Play-In Tournament's lower bracket. Despite the historic defeat, Mosley expressed confidence his message was still connecting. "You're not as bad as you think you are in your losses, and you're not as great as you think you are in your wins," he said. "We've got to make sure we get on the right mentality coming down to these last eight games."
